88.reason: [Missing bounds check: after incrementing i, the next iteration may access index out of bounds if i reaches input.length()]

139.reason: [When encountering a single '|' or '&', the program reports an error but does not consume the current character. This causes the next iteration to reprocess the same character (e.g., '|'), leading to an infinite loop or incorrect line number tracking. The index 'i' should be incremented even when reporting the error to avoid reprocessing the same invalid character.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 1.0 , reason:  [When encountering a single '|' or '&', the program reports an error but does not consume the current character. This causes the next iteration to reprocess the same character (e.g., '|'), leading to an infinite loop or incorrect line number tracking. The index 'i' should be incremented even when reporting the error to avoid reprocessing the same invalid character.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 1.0 , reason:  [When encountering a single '|' or '&', the code reports an error but does not advance the index `i` beyond the invalid character. This causes the lexer to get stuck in an infinite loop or skip processing subsequent characters incorrectly, because after reporting the error, the main loop doesn't increment `i` for the erroneous character. The current logic increments `i` before checking the next character, but if the next character is not the expected one (e.g., second '|'), it reports an error but leaves `i` pointing at the unexpected character, which will be reprocessed in the next iteration—leading to repeated error reporting or infinite loop. However, more critically, after reporting the error, the code should still consume the single '&' or '|' to avoid reprocessing it. But even more fundamentally: the code fails to handle the case where `i` reaches the end of input after incrementing (e.g., if '&' is the last character), leading to IndexOutOfBoundsException when accessing `input.charAt(i)`.]
